comments.author: unknown
comments.text: hi kaitlyn! I believe you didn’t mean any harm by this video. As a fellow Asian person from Irvine, I totally get the privilege being raised in a comfortable environment like Irvine and peers taking that for granted. Kids from irvine are spoiled af they talk like their mom’s money isn’t paying for their education. Though, it is important to know the reason why irvine is the way it is: ethnic and economic discrimination. Irvine tries to price out other minorities with steep housing costs, and it’s a well known fact that irvine is homelessness-free because the unhoused are displaced in neighboring areas like tustin and santa ana. If you want to get into the nitty gritty of it, the reason why we haven’t been affected by the Garden Grove pollutant leak is because companies will specifically place toxic gas tanks in lower-income areas so they won’t affect richer areas (us). It’s heavy to say, but our privilege of living here is built on discrimination against other minorities and the working class. That being said, I know you didn’t mean any harm, but I hope you can understand where some of the comments are coming from!

comments.author: unknown
comments.text: hi everyone, first off, i didn’t expect my video to get this many views and opinions. i apologize if this video came off as insensitive to people but this video is not a take. this video’s purpose was solely to share my own personal experience seen through the eyes of someone who has had a more positive experience in irvine. yes, the end of my video could have been more well thought out or kept out entirely because everyone has different experiences in irvine. so i apologize and appreciate everyone leaving their own criticisms and thoughts out for me to read. i am very privileged and grateful to have had the experience i have in irvine currently and understand not everyone has the same experience as me. everyone is entitled to their own opinion and whether it is positive or negative is not up to me. once again this is my personal experience and it’s important to remember not everyone will have the same experiences as each other.

comments.author: unknown
comments.text: Can I ask when you grew up? Like what years were you in elementary through high school? I ask because the demographics have changed a lot since I was a kid. Irvine’s always had plenty of Asians but it wasn’t THIS homogenous until the last decade or so. When I was growing up my neighborhood was super diverse. I could name a handful of people from every ethnic group. But over time I’m noticing majority East Asian and less of everything else. The businesses in my area reflect this as well. The boba shops, H-Marts, and ramen restaurants near my house used to be pizza places, Italian shaved ice, an Albertsons, and a French bakery. So it’s possible that you and I had two very different experiences of growing up Asian in Irvine depending on the year. For reference, I’m 29 y/o and I was in elem/high school from 2002-2015.

comments.author: unknown
comments.text: As an Ohio Asian coming from a hometown of 0.3% Asian population, it’s so interesting and insightful to hear your experience. I live in socal now and it’s so crazy the difference. I will say tho that growing up in a non-Asian dominated place, I’ve learned learned a lot of life lessons and gained appreciation for my Asian culture and things bc it was so scarce where I’m from. But I’m sure there’s also probably many lessons you’ve learned growing up this way too! The Asian American experience is not a one size fits all and that’s what’s so eye-opening. We all learn from eachother!

comments.author: unknown
comments.text: As a Houston Asian, I feel like my experience is somewhere in the middle of two extremes, but maybe still leaning more Asian tho. I wasn’t the only Asian kid, but I also wasn’t one in an overwhelmingly homogeneous Asian American society either. The latter sounds nice, and I’ve been to Irvine/Garden Grove/Westminister and I see the depth of asianness there and all the conveniences. But growing up in HTX, I experienced beautiful and rich diversity everywhere, while still feeling like I lived in a very big Asian city. We have all the things she listed in the video too, just a little more sprawled out maybe, but there’s never a lack of access to Asian things. While Irvine sounds nice, I can’t help but feel like it’s an echo chamber of the same thoughts, opinions, and personalities when you live in that type of homogeneous society. There’s value and beauty in growing up in diverse communities.

comments.author: unknown
comments.text: I can confirm everything in this video as a fellow Irvine Asian 🥹🥹 so so grateful to live here but my heart truly does go out to all the Asian Americans who’ve never felt a sense of belonging in their area just bc of their race, genuinely nobody should have to experience that and I’m forever lucky to be raised in a predominantly Asian area surrounded by families similar to mine 🙏 some ppl here don’t realize their privilege tho which is insane to me

comments.text: Anomie is a sociological concept defined as the breakdown or absence of social norms, values, and moral guidance in an individual or society. Derived from the Greek word anomos (meaning "lawlessness"), it describes a state of normlessness where people feel disconnected, disoriented, and lacking clear purpose.
